The first season of Lock Upp, which aired on Alt Balaji and MX Player, attracted considerable attention for its unfiltered format, where celebrities are confined in a jail-like setting and compete for survival. Its return has been anticipated since the original wrapped with strong ratings.

With the contestant lineup now beginning to take shape, the show's producers will be hoping the new cast can generate the same level of public engagement that made Season 1 a talking point across social media and mainstream entertainment coverage.
key_facts: Akanksha Chamola, Dheeraj Dhoopar, Sunita Ahuja confirmed as contestants
Lock Upp is hosted by Kangana Ranaut
Show is in its second season

stories.headline: Guru Randhawa Represents India at Paris Fashion Week's Front Row
stories.what: Singer Guru Randhawa made an appearance at Paris Fashion Week, sitting front row and bringing what The Times of India describes as 'Indian flair' to one of the world's most prestigious fashion events. Randhawa joins a growing number of Indian entertainers making their presence felt at international fashion weeks, reflecting the rising global profile of Indian pop culture. His appearance is likely to draw attention both in India and among fashion circles abroad.
stories.lens: Indian musicians regularly front-rowing at Paris Fashion Week signals how Bollywood and Indian pop culture figures are now courted by global luxury fashion brands as credible style ambassadors.
stories.article:
Singer Guru Randhawa attended Paris Fashion Week as a front-row guest, bringing a distinctly Indian presence to one of the fashion world's most watched events. The Times of India reported his appearance, noting the 'Indian flair' he brought to the occasion — an acknowledgment of how Indian celebrity culture is increasingly intersecting with global high fashion.

Randhawa, known for a string of chart-topping Punjabi pop and Bollywood numbers, joins a wave of Indian entertainers — from actors to musicians to influencers — who have in recent seasons been invited to fashion weeks in Paris, Milan, and New York. The trend reflects the growing commercial importance of the Indian market to luxury fashion houses and their interest in Indian cultural ambassadors.

Front-row placement at Paris Fashion Week carries significant symbolic weight in the industry, representing an invitation from a fashion house to be seen as part of its extended cultural universe. For Indian celebrities, such appearances also translate into extensive social media coverage back home, amplifying brand associations.

Randhawa's presence is a small but telling marker of how Indian entertainment figures are expanding their footprint into the global lifestyle and fashion space, at a moment when India's consumer market and cultural influence are commanding serious international attention.
key_facts: Guru Randhawa appeared front row at Paris Fashion Week
Described as bringing 'Indian flair' to the event
Reported by The Times of India

stories.headline: Baahubali Was Called 'Indian Cinema's Biggest Disaster' on Release Day, Reveals SS Rajamouli
stories.what: Director SS Rajamouli has revealed that Baahubali faced a deficit of ₹70 crore on its release day and was labelled 'Indian cinema's biggest disaster' by industry insiders at the time. The disclosure offers a startling behind-the-scenes look at how close one of India's most celebrated blockbusters came to being written off. Rajamouli's candid admission underlines how dramatically the film's fortunes reversed to become a landmark of Indian cinema.
stories.lens: A ₹70 crore opening-day deficit on what became India's biggest franchise is a reminder of how brutally unpredictable the film business can be — even for an eventual blockbuster.
stories.article:
Baahubali faced a deficit of ₹70 crore on its very release day and was being called 'Indian cinema's biggest disaster' by those tracking its early box-office performance, director SS Rajamouli has revealed. The admission, made in a recent interview carried by Hindustan Times, is a striking contrast to the film's eventual status as one of the highest-grossing Indian productions of all time.

Rajamouli's account paints a picture of a deeply anxious opening period for the magnum-opus period drama. Despite years of production, a massive budget, and enormous pre-release buzz, the film's initial collections apparently failed to meet industry expectations, triggering alarm among stakeholders and prompting sweeping write-offs from trade observers.

The turnaround that followed became the stuff of Bollywood and Tollywood legend. Word-of-mouth, repeat viewings, and the cliffhanger ending of Part 1 drove collections upward in the days and weeks after release, ultimately reshaping what Indian cinema believed was commercially possible. The sequel, Baahubali: The Conclusion, went on to smash records across the country.

Rajamouli's frank recollection is a rare glimpse into the pressure filmmakers absorb behind the spectacle. It also serves as a timely reminder — at a moment when the Indian film industry is keenly debating big-budget viability — that even the most celebrated blockbusters are not immune to the terror of opening day.
key_facts: Baahubali had a deficit of ₹70 crore on its release day
Film was labelled 'Indian cinema's biggest disaster' initially
Revelation made by director SS Rajamouli
Film later became one of India's highest-grossing productions
stories.image_credit: Dani Charles Silverscreen Media Inc. (https://silverscreen.in) / Wikimedia Commons

stories.headline: Millie Bobby Brown Says She Wants to Star in an Indian Comedy Film: 'I'm a Funny Girl'
stories.what: Stranger Things star Millie Bobby Brown has expressed a desire to appear in an Indian comedy film, declaring 'I'm a funny girl' in remarks reported by Hindustan Times. The comment, made in the context of her admiration for Indian cinema, is likely to delight her substantial Indian fanbase and has sparked discussion about the growing global curiosity around Bollywood and Indian film industries. Brown is currently one of Hollywood's most bankable young stars.
stories.lens: A global Hollywood star publicly angling for a Bollywood comedy role underscores how Indian cinema's international profile has risen to the point of attracting top-tier Western talent.
stories.article:
Hollywood star Millie Bobby Brown has declared her wish to act in an Indian comedy film, saying 'I'm a funny girl' in an interview reported by Hindustan Times. The remark is a playful but pointed expression of interest from one of the most recognisable young actors in global entertainment, and is certain to generate excitement among her large Indian fanbase.

Brown, best known for her role as Eleven in Netflix's Stranger Things, has built a following that spans continents, with India representing one of the show's largest international audiences. Her expressed interest in Bollywood — specifically in comedy — signals an awareness of the Indian film industry that goes beyond the usual generic admiration.

The comments come at a moment when cross-cultural collaborations between Hollywood and Indian cinema are increasingly discussed, even if relatively rare in practice. Indian studios and streaming platforms have been actively expanding their global ambitions, and the idea of a major Western star crossing over into a Hindi or regional language comedy would represent a significant milestone.

Whether Brown's remarks translate into anything concrete remains to be seen, but the enthusiasm is genuine enough to have put the idea firmly into public conversation — and likely into the inboxes of a few Bollywood producers.
key_facts: Millie Bobby Brown wants to star in an Indian comedy film
Said 'I'm a funny girl' explaining her interest
Brown is best known for Stranger Things on Netflix
